children.value: This page will become the dedicated treatment of stochastic processes, covering everything that random
) deliberately leaves out: time indexing, autocorrelation, stationarity, power spectral density, and ergodicity.
The main probability series (parts 1–6 + applied_sensors) only needs single random variables, so this material is not on the critical path for those parts. Read it after part 0 once the question
“what about a whole window of samples, not just one?”
starts to matter — typically when you reach Part II (Signals and Measurement) or any time-series work.
(single trial, no time index) and notes in passing that a
is the time-indexed version. That distinction is enough to keep parts 1–6 honest, but it leaves real questions on the table:
What does it mean for two samples in a vibration stream to be “related”?
Why does a 1 s window of accelerometer data look noticeably different from a 10 s window of the same machine?
How do you describe a noise signal whose statistics drift over an exposure?
What’s actually being measured by a power spectral density plot?
tell you the same thing as averaging
Each of these needs the language of stochastic processes. This page builds that language from first principles.
The sections below are placeholders. Each will follow the same applied-first pattern as the rest of the probability series:
one concrete sensor scenario → the math needed to describe it → numbers → cross-domain examples.

A short tour of the named processes that show up everywhere in signal processing and
— pink, brown, etc.; PSD shapes you’ll see in real sensors
— cumulative sum of white noise; non-stationary, integrated noise
— continuous-time random walk; appears in finance, diffusion models, SDE-based generative models
— discrete-state, memoryless transitions; foundational for HMMs and reinforcement learning

8. Where Stochastic Processes Show Up Downstream

children.label: 8. Where Stochastic Processes Show Up Downstream

children.value: PSD-based fault detection; stationarity checks for “is the machine state changing?”
PSD, spectrograms, noise modelling for speech enhancement
Fixed-pattern noise vs read noise vs shot noise — different temporal correlation structure
Stationarity is a prerequisite for ARIMA; autocorrelation drives model order
The forward process is a Wiener process; the reverse process is a learned SDE
Linear-Gaussian stochastic processes are the entire substrate
